CONFIGURED-panel review (one of 2; return verdict JSON, write NOTHING). Round 1.
Task: T90 (goals:G4) — "Re-bind a roamed source to its peer on a fresh authenticated PROBE".
acceptance: Roam test — peer B's source changes; B's DATA from the new source is dropped until an authenticated PROBE under B's psk re-binds it, after which it routes to B; throughout, peer A's resequencer never observes any of B's frames. `go test ./internal/bind/...` passes.
Worktree: /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-ab151251d328f46db (branch implement/T90, base 55ff983, HEAD 8c92b95). Go repo — gate via `nix develop -c sh -c '...'`, incl. `go test -race ./internal/bind/...`.
Worker: pass; test-only diff (internal/bind/concentrator_roam_test.go). Claim: the T88/T89 unbound-source gate + PROBE-only bindSourceToPeer already satisfy the roam property, so T90 is a behavior-locking test not a production fix; mutation-verified vs two breakages.
Scrutinize: (a) does the test model a genuine ROAM — peer B ALREADY bound to source S1, then B's traffic appears from a NEW source S2 — distinct from the plain unbound-source case? (b) is the "DATA dropped until fresh PROBE" window actually exercised (DATA from S2 arrives BEFORE any PROBE from S2 and is asserted dropped)? (c) is peer A's resequencer non-observation asserted throughout? (d) INDEPENDENTLY mutation-test: make a roamed source's non-PROBE DATA bind/dispatch and confirm the test fails. If the roam-specific case is NOT actually distinguished from T89's coverage, that is a real criticism. Return { taskId, verdict, criticism: [], questions: [], defects: [], rationale, summary }.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.

TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.

Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. 1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"bytes"
5		"testing"
6		"time"
7	
8		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/fec"
9		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/frame"
10		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
11	)
12	
13	// fecReceiveConfig is the fixed-ratio group the strengthened PARITY subtest arms both
14	// peers' receivers with. K=1 parity is all that is needed: a DataCount=1 group (one data
15	// shard) reconstructs that sole data frame from the single parity shard ALONE, so a PARITY
16	// that leaks past the demux gate into dispatchInbound recovers a data frame straight into
17	// the dispatching peer's resequencer — which is what makes the leak observable.
18	var fecReceiveConfig = fec.Config{DataShards: 4, ParityShards: 1, Deadline: time.Millisecond}
19	
20	// enableFECReceive arms a peer's receive-side FEC through the SAME seam Open uses
21	// (openPeerDatapathLocked, multipath.go:851) to Store a fecReceiver. Without it the Parity
22	// case of dispatchInbound is a no-op (fecRecv nil), so a leaked PARITY would touch no
23	// resequencer and the drop property this subtest names would be unobservable.
24	func enableFECReceive(t testing.TB, p *peerState, cfg fec.Config) {
25		t.Helper()
26		dec, err := fec.NewDecoder(cfg)
27		if err != nil {
28			t.Fatalf("build FEC decoder: %v", err)
29		}
30		p.fecRecv.Store(&fecReceiver{dec: dec, connLoss: telemetry.NewConnLoss(fecResidualLossWindow)})
31	}
32	
33	// singleFrameParity builds a GENUINE parity shard for a one-data-frame group whose sole
34	// data shard's coded bytes are seq||inner (fecShardPayload) — the exact shape the datapath
35	// codes parity over. Offered to a decoder built at the same cfg, this parity ALONE
36	// reconstructs that data frame, so a PARITY carrying it would deliver seq||inner up a
37	// resequencer at outer-seq `seq`. It returns the wire fields for a frame.Parity.
38	func singleFrameParity(t testing.TB, cfg fec.Config, seq uint64, inner []byte) (group uint32, index uint16, dataCount uint8, payload []byte) {
39		t.Helper()
40		enc, err := fec.NewEncoder(cfg, fec.SystemClock{})
41		if err != nil {
42			t.Fatalf("build FEC encoder: %v", err)
43		}
44		if _, _, aerr := enc.Admit(fecShardPayload(seq, inner)); aerr != nil {
45			t.Fatalf("admit single data shard: %v", aerr)
46		}
47		parity, err := enc.Flush() // close the 1-data group, coding K parity over its sole shard
48		if err != nil {
49			t.Fatalf("flush single-frame group: %v", err)
50		}
51		if len(parity) != cfg.ParityShards {
52			t.Fatalf("single-frame group emitted %d parity, want %d", len(parity), cfg.ParityShards)
53		}
54		pshard := parity[0]
55		if pshard.DataCount != 1 {
56			t.Fatalf("single-frame parity DataCount = %d, want 1", pshard.DataCount)
57		}
58		return uint32(pshard.Group), uint16(pshard.Index), uint8(pshard.DataCount), pshard.Payload
59	}
60	
61	// TestSharedSocketGatesUnboundDataParity is the T89 acceptance: on a SHARED (multi-view)
62	// concentrator socket, DATA and PARITY arriving from a source with no established
63	// source->peer binding must reach NO peer's resequencer — dropped outright — EVEN when the
64	// frame validly decodes under some peer's codec (a real peer's psk, so the MAC-less
65	// DATA/PARITY body deciphers cleanly under that psk). A DATA/PARITY frame carries no binding
66	// authority (D9/D11: only an authenticated PROBE binds a source), so a garble-decode under
67	// any codec must never attribute the frame to that peer. After an authenticated PROBE binds
68	// the source to peer B, subsequent DATA/PARITY from it route to B's plane only.
69	//
70	// This hardens the T88 multi-peer gate: T88 already `continue`s past a non-PROBE trial-decode
71	// so an unbound DATA/PARITY falls through to the loop-end drop; this test pins that property
72	// down for BOTH kinds and asserts NEITHER peer's resequencer is touched (not merely peer B's).
73	func TestSharedSocketGatesUnboundDataParity(t *testing.T) {
74		pskA := testKey(t, 0x11) // primary
75		pskB := testKey(t, 0x22) // second peer
76	
77		// codecB deciphers exactly as peer B's per-path view codec does (same psk), so a frame it
78		// encodes is guaranteed to VALIDLY decode under peer B's view during the demux trial — the
79		// discriminating case the acceptance calls out ("encode it under a real peer's psk").
80		codecB, err := frame.NewCodec(pskB)
81		if err != nil {
82			t.Fatalf("build peer B codec: %v", err)
83		}
84	
85		t.Run("DATA that validly decodes under peer B's psk from an unbound source reaches no resequencer", func(t *testing.T) {
86			m, primary, second := twoPeerConcentrator(t, pskA, pskB)
87			secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
88			_, peerAP := rawPeer(t) // a fresh, unbound source address
89	
90			data, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: 5, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: []byte("unbound-data")})
91			if err != nil {
92				t.Fatalf("encode DATA under psk B: %v", err)
93			}
94			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], data, peerAP)
95	
96			// Dropped: neither peer's resequencer received it, and no binding was minted.
97			if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
98				t.Fatalf("DATA from an UNBOUND source landed in peer B's resequencer (%q); a valid decode must not attribute it", it.Payload)
99			}
100			if it, ok := primary.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
101				t.Fatalf("DATA from an UNBOUND source landed in the PRIMARY resequencer (%q)", it.Payload)
102			}
103			if _, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr()); ok {
104				t.Fatal("an unbound DATA frame established a source->peer binding (D9/D11 violated)")
105			}
106		})
107

160	// window must be positive; timeout may be zero (a missing head seq is skipped as
161	// soon as the next Observe/Pop advances the clock past the arrival instant).
162	func New(window uint64, timeout time.Duration, clock Clock) *Resequencer {
163		if window == 0 {
164			panic("reseq: window must be positive")
165		}
166		if clock == nil {
167			panic("reseq: clock must be non-nil")
168		}
169		return &Resequencer{
170			window:  window,
171			timeout: timeout,
172			clock:   clock,
173			ring:    make([]slot, window),
174		}
175	}
176	
177	// Observe ingests one DATA frame's inner payload under its outer-seq. It takes
178	// ownership of payload (the caller must not mutate it afterwards); the multipath
179	// Bind passes the freshly-decoded frame.Data.Payload, which aliases nothing else.
180	// src is the outer source address the frame arrived from. Any frames that become
181	// deliverable are appended to the ready FIFO for Pop.
182	func (r *Resequencer) Observe(seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) {
183		now := r.clock.Now()
184		r.mu.Lock()
185		defer r.mu.Unlock()
186	
187		if !r.started {
188			r.started = true
189			r.next = seq
190		}
191	
192		// Give up on any head-of-line gap whose hold time has elapsed before deciding
193		// where this frame lands.
194		r.expire(now)
195	
196		// Classify the frame against the release window and the discontinuity guard.
197		// admit adjusts next (window-advance or resync) when warranted and reports
198		// whether the frame now lands inside [next, next+window) and should be placed.
199		if !r.admit(seq) {
200			return
201		}
202	
203		cell := &r.ring[seq%r.window]
204		if cell.occupied && cell.seq == seq {
205			r.dropDup++
206			return
207		}
208		cell.seq = seq
209		cell.src = src
210		cell.payload = payload
211		cell.occupied = true
212		r.buf++
213		if r.buf > r.highWater {
214			r.highWater = r.buf
215		}
216	
217		r.drain()
218		r.arm(now)
219	}
220	
221	// ObserveRecovered ingests a payload the FEC decoder reconstructed from parity (T24).
222	// Unlike Observe it NEVER moves or re-pins the release point: a recovered frame is a
223	// REPAIR for a past gap, so if it has already fallen below the release point it is
224	// dropped as too-late, and it can neither window-advance nor corroborate a resync — so
225	// FEC recovery can never CAUSE the burst loss it exists to prevent (a late batch of K
226	// recovered seqs below `next` must not be able to dump the live buffer via a backward
227	// resync). It returns whether the frame was PLACED (landed at or above the release
228	// point and will be delivered in order); a dropped-late recovered frame returns false,
229	// which the datapath uses to keep the /metrics recovered counter honest — counting
230	// frames actually delivered ahead of the release point, not merely reconstructed.
231	func (r *Resequencer) ObserveRecovered(seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) bool {
232		now := r.clock.Now()
233		r.mu.Lock()
234		defer r.mu.Unlock()
235	
236		if !r.started {
237			r.started = true
238			r.next = seq
239		}
240		r.expire(now)
241	
242		// The placement window is EXACTLY [next, next+window). A recovered frame below next
243		// is too late (drop, and — crucially — never feed the resync guard). One at or beyond
244		// next+window would require a window-advance we refuse to perform for a reconstructed
245		// frame, since that would skip live seqs on the strength of a repair. Neither branch
246		// touches next or the corroboration run.
247		if seq < r.next {
248			if r.next-seq > r.window {
249				r.dropSuspect++
250			} else {
251				r.dropLate++
252			}
253			return false
254		}
255		if seq-r.next >= r.window {
256			r.dropLate++
257			return false
258		}
259	
260		cell := &r.ring[seq%r.window]
261		if cell.occupied && cell.seq == seq {
262			r.dropDup++
263			return false // already buffered (a received frame or a prior recovery)
264		}
265		cell.seq = seq
266		cell.src = src
267		cell.payload = payload
268		cell.occupied = true
269		r.buf++
270		if r.buf > r.highWater {
271			r.highWater = r.buf
272		}
273	
274		r.drain()
275		r.arm(now)
276		return true
277	}
